Gene_Accession_Number.reason: The paper explicitly states 'SU1 (CYP105A1) gene' from Streptomyces griseolus.

Source_Species.reason: Explicitly described as isolated from the soil bacterium Streptomyces griseolus.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The Latin binomial Streptomyces griseolus is given in the text.

Applied_Species.reason: The paper states SU1 was expressed in tobacco.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper says tobacco, but does not explicitly provide the Latin name Nicotiana tabacum.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: SU1 is described as a bacterial P450 and specifically CYP105A1, i.e. a cytochrome P450 monooxygenase.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The text says a chloroplast transit sequence was added to target SU1 to the chloroplast stroma, where ferredoxin provides redox partner.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The review says SU1 activates proherbicide R7402 via N-dealkylation and also detoxifies some sulfonylureas with low efficiency, but it does not specify the exact chemical name of the activated product or detailed sulfonylurea metabolites.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: The products discussed are herbicide/proherbicide-derived xenobiotic metabolites.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Explicitly stated: chloroplast-targeted SU1 activates harmless R7402 into a highly phytotoxic herbicide; whole-plant expression causes death, tapetum-specific expression causes male sterility; used for negative selection.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: The paper describes transgenic expression in tobacco, chloroplast targeting with transit sequence, tapetum-specific promoter use, and R7402 treatment phenotyping.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three summarized points are directly described in the review.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The review supports the functional consequence—activation to confer selectable toxicity/negative selection and conditional male sterility—even though the exact metabolite identity is not named.

Applied_Promoters.reason: The paper states expression under promoters directing ectopic expression and a promoter specific for the tapetum.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The review explicitly says the SU1/R7402 system is widely used as a negative selection marker and describes male-sterility application.

Other_Important_Info.reason: This is indeed a review article summarizing SU1 as an applied herbicide-metabolizing P450 rather than newly discovering it.

Source_Species.reason: The paper explicitly refers to 'rat CYP1A1'.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The species is given only as rat; the Latin name Rattus norvegicus is not stated in the review.

Applied_Species.reason: The review says herbicide-tolerant tobacco and potato plants were generated using rat CYP1A1.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper names tobacco and potato but does not explicitly give Nicotiana tabacum and Solanum tuberosum.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: CYP1A1 is described as a mammalian P450 used for herbicide metabolism.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The review says fusion proteins were targeted to the chloroplast and that the fusion protein located in thylakoid membranes showed enhanced activity under light; it also notes microsomal origin for mammalian P450s by implication, though not for the construct directly.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The text supports hydroxylated/dealkylated metabolites for atrazine and chlortoluron, but pyriminobac-methyl is only listed among herbicides metabolized and its exact metabolite type is not specified.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The paper states CYP1A1 was exploited to generate herbicide-tolerant tobacco and potato plants.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Supported by the described transgenic expression in tobacco and potato, fusion to yeast P450 reductase, chloroplast targeting, and herbicide tolerance assays.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each point is described: broad herbicide metabolism including atrazine/chlortoluron/pyriminobac-methyl; ring-methyl hydroxylation and N-dealkylation; transgenic herbicide tolerance improved by reductase fusion/chloroplast targeting.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The metabolites are discussed in the context of herbicide detoxification and crop protection/tolerance.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The review presents CYP1A1 as a proof-of-concept tool for engineering herbicide tolerance, with ethical caveat.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Explicitly supported: the review raises ethical concerns because CYP1A1 can activate polyaromatic hydrocarbons into procarcinogens.

Other_Important_Info.reason: It is indeed presented as a core engineering example in this review.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The paper identifies CYP76B1 as a P450 gene/protein from Jerusalem artichoke, but does not explicitly use the exact phrase 'Cytochrome P450 76B1 monooxygenase'.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: The review explicitly names the gene CYP76B1.

Source_Species.reason: Explicitly stated to be a gene from Jerusalem artichoke (Helianthus tuberosus).

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The Latin name Helianthus tuberosus is explicitly given.

Applied_Species.reason: The review states CYP76B1 was over-expressed in tobacco.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper gives tobacco but not the Latin name Nicotiana tabacum.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The gene is discussed in the context of herbicide detoxification/xenobiotic metabolism.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Explicitly supports phenylurea detoxification via dealkylation, including double dealkylation products for phenylurea.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: CYP76B1 directly participates in detoxification of herbicides.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: The review says phenylurea are converted to non-phytotoxic di-N-dealkylated products.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Supported by text stating CYP76B1 dealkylates several planar xenobiotics including phenylurea and converts them to non-phytotoxic di-N-dealkylated products.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Chlortoluron and several phenylurea herbicides are explicitly discussed; linuron resistance is also mentioned.

Primary_Product.reason: The paper supports di-N-dealkylated phenylurea products for phenylurea herbicides.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Explicitly described as non-phytotoxic di-N-dealkylated phenylurea metabolites.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper directly ties these products to herbicide detoxification and increased tolerance.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Plant P450s are generally described as ER-bound, but CYP76B1-specific localization is not directly demonstrated in the review.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: CYP76B1 is clearly a cytochrome P450 monooxygenase in the CYP76 family.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: The review explicitly says CYP76B1 was expressed in yeast together with an Arabidopsis P450 reductase.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Explicitly stated to be inducible by Mn2+ ions, aminopyrine, and phenobarbital.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: These inducers are explicitly mentioned; herbicide treatment context is also discussed.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are directly described: isolation based on inducibility, yeast expression with Arabidopsis reductase, activity assays, tobacco overexpression, tolerance phenotyping.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The review does not state that CYP76B1 is a rate-limiting or key control step.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three summary statements are supported by the review text and figure legend.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The review states overexpression in tobacco increases tolerance to chlortoluron and linuron via detoxification.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper presents CYP76B1 as one of the more efficient plant herbicide-metabolizing P450s and therefore a promising engineering candidate, though 'acceptability' vs mammalian genes is an inference.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The review explicitly notes increased resistance to linuron and presents CYP76B1 as among the more efficient plant P450s tested.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The review names CYP71A10 but does not explicitly use the full phrase 'Cytochrome P450 71A10 monooxygenase'.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: The gene is explicitly identified as CYP71A10.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Soybean is mentioned, but the Latin name Glycine max is not explicitly provided in the review.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: It is discussed as part of herbicide detoxification/xenobiotic metabolism.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Supported: phenylurea detoxification by ring-methyl hydroxylation and mono-N-demethylation.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: CYP71A10 directly catalyzes detoxification-related oxidative steps.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: The paper supports direct formation of herbicide metabolites linked to tolerance, but one product for chlortoluron is mono-N-demethylated and can remain phytotoxic in general context; calling all products 'detoxification products' overstates certainty.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Exactly supported by the review: chlortoluron gives ring-methyl hydroxylated and to lesser extent mono-N-demethylated products; other phenylurea are mainly mono-N-demethylated.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Chlortoluron and other phenylurea herbicides are explicitly discussed.

Primary_Product.reason: Supported by the described ring-methyl hydroxylated and mono-N-demethylated products.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The review supports hydroxylated and mono-N-demethylated phenylurea metabolites.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Associated with increased herbicide tolerance, though the mono-N-demethylated chlortoluron product is elsewhere described as potentially phytotoxic; the broad functional claim is therefore not fully secure for every metabolite.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Plant P450s are generally ER-bound, but CYP71A10-specific localization is not directly shown in the paper.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: CYP71A10 is a cytochrome P450 in the CYP71 family.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Phenylurea herbicide exposure is part of the assay context, but not explicitly described as a regulatory factor for expression of CYP71A10.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Supported: isolated using P450-conserved sequences, expressed in yeast, metabolites profiled, overexpressed in tobacco, tolerance assessed.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: No rate-limiting role is established in the review.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three points are directly supported, including lower efficiency than CYP76B1 and tolerance increase in tobacco.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Supported: overexpression increases tolerance to phenylurea herbicides.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: As a plant gene conferring increased phenylurea tolerance, it is a reasonable candidate, but this application value is more inferred than explicitly stated.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The review explicitly says catalytic turnover with phenylurea is more than ten times lower than that of CYP76B1.
